perturbation.original_text: An “Affiliate” means any person, partnership, joint venture,corporation or other form of enterprise, domestic or foreign, including but notlimited to subsidiaries, that directly or indirectly, control, are controlled by, orare under common control with a party.
perturbation.changed_text: An “Affiliate” means any person, partnership, joint venture,corporation or other form of enterprise, domestic or foreign, including but notlimited to subsidiaries, that directly or indirectly, control, are controlled by, orare under common control with a party. However, for the purposes of Section 2(a)(i), an affiliate shall only include subsidiaries directly owned by the Receiving Party.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces an in-text contradiction by defining 'Affiliate' differently for the entire agreement versus specifically for Section 2(a)(i). The original definition applies broadly, while the modified text narrows the definition for the purpose of the clause regarding the duration of non-disclosure obligations. This will create an in-text contradiction by making it unclear which entities are considered affiliates for that clause, leading to potential disputes regarding the duration of confidentiality obligations. For example, a company might argue that a sister company is not a directly owned subsidiary and, therefore, not subject to the ten-year non-disclosure term outlined in 2(a)(i).
perturbation.contradicted_text: (a) Receiving Party shall: (i) Refrain from disclosing any Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party to third parties for ten (10) years following the date that DisclosingParty first discloses such Confidential Information to Receiving Party, forany reason whatsoever, except as expressly provided in Sections 2(b) and2(c) of this Agreement; (ii) Take reasonable security precautions, at least as great as the precautions it takes to protect its own Confidential Information, but no less thanreasonable care, to keep confidential the Confidential Information of theDisclosing Party; (iii) Refrain from disclosing, reproducing, summarizing and/or distributing Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party except in pursuance ofReceiving Party’s business relationship with Disclosing Party, and only asotherwise provided hereunder; and (iv) Refrain from reverse engineering, decompiling or disassembling any software code and/or pre-release hardware devices disclosed by DisclosingParty to Receiving Party under the terms of this Agreement, except asexpressly permitted by applicable law.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the definition of 'Affiliate' specifically for Section 2(a)(i), narrowing it to only include directly owned subsidiaries. This directly contradicts the broader definition of 'Affiliate' used throughout the rest of the agreement, including the original and broader application of Section 2(a)(i). This creates an in-text contradiction because it becomes unclear which entities are considered affiliates for the purpose of the ten-year non-disclosure term, leading to potential disputes and making the agreement inconsistent and unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: (a) Receiving Party shall: (i) Refrain from disclosing any Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party to third parties for ten (10) years following the date that DisclosingParty first discloses such Confidential Information to Receiving Party, forany reason whatsoever, except as expressly provided in Sections 2(b) and2(c) of this Agreement;
perturbation.changed_text: (a) Receiving Party shall: (i) Refrain from disclosing any Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party to third parties for five (5) years following the date that DisclosingParty first discloses such Confidential Information to Receiving Party, forany reason whatsoever, except as expressly provided in Sections 2(b) and2(c) of this Agreement;
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces an in-text contradiction by changing the non-disclosure period from ten (10) years to five (5) years in Section 2(a)(i). While the original text states a 10-year period, the new text shortens this to 5 years. This will contradict the later statement in Section 4(j), which references that the obligations concerning Confidential Information disclosed during the term of the Agreement shall survive termination of the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: (j) Either party may terminate this Agreement with or without cause upon ninety (90) days prior written notice to the other party. All sections of this Agreementrelating to the rights and obligations of the parties concerning ConfidentialInformation disclosed during the term of the Agreement shall survive any suchtermination.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the non-disclosure period from ten (10) years to five (5) years. Section 4(j) states that obligations concerning Confidential Information disclosed during the term of the Agreement shall survive termination. Because the non-disclosure period is shortened in the changed text, the survival obligation in Section 4(j) now contradicts the duration of confidentiality, creating a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: (c) The undersigned Receiving Party may disclose Confidential Information only to Receiving Party’s employees and consultants on a need-to-know basis. Theundersigned Receiving Party will have executed or shall execute appropriatewritten agreements with its employees and consultants sufficient to enable it tocomply with all the provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: (c) The undersigned Receiving Party may disclose Confidential Information only to Receiving Party’s employees and consultants on a need-to-know basis, except interns who can access all confidential information for training purposes. Theundersigned Receiving Party will have executed or shall execute appropriatewritten agreements with its employees and consultants sufficient to enable it tocomply with all the provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This change creates an in-text contradiction regarding who can access confidential information. The original clause restricts disclosure to employees and consultants on a need-to-know basis. By adding an exception for interns with blanket access ('except interns who can access all confidential information for training purposes'), the clause now contradicts itself. This directly contradicts the overarching principle of restricting access and potentially violates the confidentiality intended by the Disclosing Party. This will create uncertainty in enforcement, as it is unclear what level of protection the Receiving Party must provide to the Confidential Information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: (a) Receiving Party shall: (i) Refrain from disclosing any Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party to third parties for ten (10) years following the date that DisclosingParty first discloses such Confidential Information to Receiving Party, forany reason whatsoever, except as expressly provided in Sections 2(b) and2(c) of this Agreement; (ii) Take reasonable security precautions, at least as great as the precautions it takes to protect its own Confidential Information, but no less thanreasonable care, to keep confidential the Confidential Information of theDisclosing Party; (iii) Refrain from disclosing, reproducing, summarizing and/or distributing Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party except in pursuance ofReceiving Party’s business relationship with Disclosing Party, and only asotherwise provided hereunder; and (iv) Refrain from reverse engineering, decompiling or disassembling any software code and/or pre-release hardware devices disclosed by DisclosingParty to Receiving Party under the terms of this Agreement, except asexpressly permitted by applicable law.
perturbation.justification: The changed text adds an exception for interns, allowing them access to all confidential information, while the original text restricts access to employees and consultants on a need-to-know basis. The contradicted text (a) states that the Receiving Party shall refrain from disclosing Confidential Information to third parties for any reason whatsoever, except as expressly provided in Sections 2(b) and 2(c), thus, the addition of interns having full access contradicts this restriction, creating a direct conflict regarding who can access confidential information.

perturbation.original_text: (e) Receiving Party shall, at Disclosing Party’s request, return all originals, copies, reproductions and summaries of Confidential Information and all other tangiblematerials and devices provided to the Receiving Party as ConfidentialInformation, or at Disclosing Party’s option, certify destruction of the same.
perturbation.changed_text: (e) Receiving Party shall, at Disclosing Party’s request, return all originals, copies, reproductions and summaries of Confidential Information and all other tangiblematerials and devices provided to the Receiving Party as ConfidentialInformation, or at Disclosing Party’s option, certify destruction of the same, unless the Receiving Party paid for the creation or development of such Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: This modification introduces an in-text contradiction by adding an exception to the return/destruction obligation in Section 2(e). The original text mandates return or destruction of Confidential Information at the Disclosing Party's request. The added clause creates an exception if the Receiving Party paid for its creation. This creates ambiguity because who owns the information if receiving party paid. This modification would contradict section 4(a) that "All Confidential Information is and shall remain the property of Disclosing Party."
perturbation.contradicted_text: (a) All Confidential Information is and shall remain the property of Disclosing Party. By disclosing Confidential Information to Receiving Party, DisclosingParty does not grant any express or implied right to Receiving Party to or underany patents, copyrights, trademarks, or trade secret information except asotherwise provided herein. Disclosing Party reserves without prejudice theability to protect its rights under any such patents, copyrights, trademarks, ortrade secrets except as otherwise provided herein.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces an exception to the return/destruction obligation of Confidential Information, stating that if the Receiving Party paid for the creation or development of such Confidential Information, they are not obligated to return or destroy it. This directly contradicts section 4(a), which states that "All Confidential Information is and shall remain the property of Disclosing Party." The exception introduced by the change implies a potential ownership right for the Receiving Party if they paid for the creation, creating a direct conflict with the original ownership assertion in section 4(a). This creates a strong, in-text contradiction as the two sections outline conflicting ideas.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement constitutes the entire agreement between the parties with respect to the subject matter hereof. It shall not be modified except by a writtenagreement dated subsequent to the date of this Agreement and signed by bothparties.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement constitutes the entire agreement between the parties with respect to the subject matter hereof. It shall not be modified except by a writtenagreement dated subsequent to the date of this Agreement and signed by bothparties. However, the Disclosing Party reserves the right to unilaterally amend this agreement with 30 days written notice to the Receiving Party.
perturbation.explanation: The introduced text contradicts the original intent by stating that the Disclosing Party can unilaterally amend the agreement. This change creates uncertainty about the agreement's stability, thus it contradicts the entire agreement clause and creates an in-text contradiction. This will create ambiguity regarding how and when future modifications will occur.
perturbation.contradicted_text: (e) This Agreement constitutes the entire agreement between the parties with respect to the subject matter hereof. It shall not be modified except by a writtenagreement dated subsequent to the date of this Agreement and signed by bothparties. None of the provisions of this Agreement shall be deemed to have beenwaived by any act or acquiescence on the part of Disclosing Party, the ReceivingParty, their agents, or employees, but only by an instrument in writing signed byan authorized employee of Disclosing Party and the Receiving Party. No waiverof any provision of this Agreement shall constitute a waiver of any otherprovision(s) or of the same provision on another occasion.
perturbation.justification: The original text and contradicted text both state that the agreement can only be modified by a written agreement signed by both parties. The changed text introduces an exception, allowing the Disclosing Party to unilaterally amend the agreement with 30 days' notice. This directly contradicts the requirement for mutual agreement in the original clause and contradicted text, creating a strong in-text contradiction as defined by the 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction' perturbation type.

perturbation.original_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement with or without cause upon ninety (90) days prior written notice to the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement with or without cause upon ninety (90) days prior written notice to the other party, unless the Disclosing Party believes the Receiving Party has disclosed confidential information, in which case the Disclosing Party may terminate the agreement immediately.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces an in-text contradiction by creating an exception to the termination clause in Section 4(j). While the original text states that the agreement can be terminated with 90 days notice, the added clause now allows immediate termination by the Disclosing Party under certain conditions. This creates uncertainty regarding when and how the agreement can be terminated, undermining the initial 90-day notice provision. This exception contradicts the set procedure for termination outlined in the first half of the sentence.
perturbation.contradicted_text: (j) Either party may terminate this Agreement with or without cause upon ninety (90) days prior written notice to the other party. All sections of this Agreementrelating to the rights and obligations of the parties concerning ConfidentialInformation disclosed during the term of the Agreement shall survive any suchtermination.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces an exception to the termination clause, allowing immediate termination by the Disclosing Party under specific conditions. This directly contradicts the original clause, which states that either party may terminate the agreement with ninety (90) days prior written notice. The contradicted text is the original termination clause that is now contradicted by the added exception. This creates an in-text contradiction regarding the procedure for termination, which falls under the 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction' perturbation type, because different sections of a contract provide conflicting obligations.
